disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Sequential triphasic colour change in the fingers - white (pallor), then blue (cyanosis), then bright red (rubor) - sharply demarcated and fully reversible
trigger_question_examples: What colours did your fingers go, and in what order?
Did the colour change have a clear edge to it?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: They went dead white first, then blue, and now they're bright red - you could see a clear line where the colour changed.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Attack precipitated by cold exposure (and reported with stress) - a vasospastic trigger
trigger_question_examples: What were you doing when it started?
Does cold or stress bring it on?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It came on right after I went out in the cold for the mail, and I've noticed it with stress too.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Bilateral, symmetric involvement of the fingers of both hands (not a single limb)
trigger_question_examples: Is it one hand or both?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It's both hands, fingers on each side, pretty much the same.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Numbness/'dead finger' sensation during the white ischaemic phase, with painful throbbing and pins-and-needles during the red recovery phase
trigger_question_examples: What did the fingers feel like when they were white?
What does it feel like now they've gone red?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: They felt dead and numb when they were white, and now they're red it's a horrible throbbing, burning, pins-and-needles pain.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No blackening, ulceration or sores of the digits; colour and warmth are currently returning (no established tissue loss)
trigger_question_examples: Are any fingers still white, black, or have sores or broken skin?
Have the fingers gone back to a more normal colour and warmth?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No black bits or sores - they're red and warm now, coming back to normal, just very painful.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Recently commenced a new daily tablet to prevent migraines (a beta-blocker) - a common precipitant/aggravator of peripheral vasospasm
trigger_question_examples: Have you started any new medications recently?
What is the new migraine tablet you started?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I started a new daily tablet from my GP a little while ago to stop my migraines - I'm not sure of the name.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Am I going to lose my fingers? This looks really bad.
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Should offer measured reassurance that a bilateral, symmetric, colour-changing attack that is already reversing (fingers going red and warm again, no black skin or sores) is typical of Raynaud's vasospasm rather than a blocked artery, while being honest that circulation and pulses cannot be fully checked over chat - so an urgent same-day in-person review is advised today to confirm the fingers are getting a normal blood supply, and immediate emergency care is needed if a finger stays white/numb or turns black.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Should I keep taking my new migraine tablet?
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Should explain that the recently started migraine-prevention tablet (a beta-blocker) can make this finger vasospasm worse and should be reviewed with the GP promptly - advising the patient to raise it urgently at the same-day/GP review rather than simply stopping a prescribed medication without medical advice; must not fabricate a dose.

deflection_behaviours.description: Patient is frightened and may either demand emergency treatment for a feared blocked artery or, once reassured, want to just wait at home - tests whether the AI Doctor calibrates triage correctly: neither over-escalating a resolving benign vasospasm to an ambulance, nor under-triaging by giving remote reassurance without in-person confirmation of perfusion and a medication review.
deflection_behaviours.deflection_text_template: If it's just my circulation overreacting to the cold, can I just warm up at home and leave it?
